title: Check yourself
questions.0.q: Your <h1> comes out big. A friend opens the same page in a different browser and the heading is a slightly different size. Who got it wrong?
questions.0.options.0: You — you should have set the size
questions.0.options.1: Nobody — you only said it was a heading; the size is the browser's decision
questions.0.options.2: Your friend — their browser is old
questions.0.options.3: The browser — it should look the same everywhere
questions.0.hint: Go back and read what you actually wrote. Is there a number in it? Is there a size at all?
questions.0.explain: You wrote <h1>. That says "first-level heading" and nothing more — no size appears anywhere in it. The size came from the user agent stylesheet every browser ships. Those files are not identical and they change over time.
questions.1.q: You forget the closing tag: you write <h1>Alex and carry on with two more lines of text below. What happens?
questions.1.options.0: The browser shows an error and stops
questions.1.options.1: Nothing — the closing tag is optional
questions.1.options.2: The heading never ends and swallows both lines below it
questions.1.hint: The browser is not a teacher. It does not stop, does not fix, does not tell you where you slipped. Think about how far it believes the heading reaches.
questions.1.explain: The browser almost never stops with an error — it tries to clean up the mess for you. An unclosed tag has no end, so it eats everything after it. The page shows, there is no error, and it is still wrong.
questions.2.q: A friend wants their sentence to be blue. What do you tell them?
questions.2.options.0: There is a tag for blue — they just have to find it
questions.2.options.1: HTML does not deal with colors; that is CSS's job
questions.2.options.2:
They should swap

for something more colorful
questions.2.options.3: The color is set in the text itself
questions.2.hint: Ask what HTML describes. What a thing IS — or how it looks?
questions.2.explain: HTML says what a thing is, not how it looks. If you are hunting for a "tag for blue", you still think you are drawing. You are describing.
questions.3.q: You press Enter three times in the code to separate two sentences. What comes out on the page?
questions.3.options.0: Three blank lines — exactly as you wrote them
questions.3.options.2: Nothing — the three Enters become one space and the sentences stick together
questions.3.options.3: An error, because blank lines confuse the page
questions.3.hint: The browser collapses every run of spaces and newlines. Down to what?
questions.3.explain: Any run of spaces, tabs and newlines becomes ONE space. That is why you cannot align with spaces — and why indentation in code is free. If you want separation, you say it with a tag.
questions.4.q: Which of these two lines is written correctly?
questions.4.options.2: Both — the browser shows them the same
questions.4.hint: Trace the order the tags open in. Then look at the order they close in.
questions.4.explain: Whatever opened last closes first. The second line crosses the tags over — the browser shows no error and guesses what you meant. Sometimes it guesses right. Sometimes not.
questions.5.q: You want more space between two paragraphs. What do you do?
questions.5.options.3: Nothing in the HTML — spacing is a look and it is made with CSS
questions.5.hint: Is spacing a matter of look or of meaning? And who is in charge of the look?
questions.5.explain:
The empty

is the most common attempt and the worst one: the screen reader announces a paragraph and reads nothing — the person waits and does not know what they missed. Spacing is a look, so it is CSS's job.
questions.6.options.2: The opening tag, the content and the closing tag together
questions.6.options.3: The text between the two tags
questions.6.hint: A tag is a part. An element is something bigger.
questions.6.explain:
is a tag.

is a tag.

Alex

is an element. The two words are not synonyms, and the difference will matter once you start talking about nested elements.
questions.7.hint: The slash has exactly one correct place. Which?
questions.7.explain: The slash goes BEFORE the tag name. <h1/> and <h1\> close nothing — the tag stays open and swallows everything after it.
questions.8.q:
You put the same sentence once in

and once in

. What is the difference?
questions.8.options.0: Only the size of the letters
questions.8.options.2:
is for important text,

for ordinary text
questions.8.options.3: The role — one is the page's heading, the other is a paragraph of text
questions.8.hint: The size is a consequence. What is the reason to pick one tag over the other?
questions.8.explain: The same words, a different role. That one comes out big and the other small is the browser's decision and it changes with CSS. The role does not change — it is the reason you picked the tag.
questions.9.q:
Why bother putting every paragraph in

when the text shows up anyway?
questions.9.options.0: Because screen readers have a shortcut to jump from paragraph to paragraph
questions.9.options.1: Because otherwise the page will not load
questions.9.options.2: Because Google does not index bare text
questions.9.options.3:
Because

makes the letters more readable
questions.9.hint: Who reads your page besides you? How do they read it if they cannot see the screen?
questions.9.explain: Paragraphs give the reader something to jump between — the way your eyes skip across blank space. Bare text has nothing to jump to and gets read letter by letter, start to finish. Google indexes bare text fine; that is not the question here.
